​@philsurtees George Lucas had executives and his wife reign him in during most of the OT. The flaws you listed came about because George was done with the franchise after a nasty divorce and a falling out with a producer. He only came back almost 20 years later when his anguish subsided and saw there was still interest in the franchise.

People also give George the benefit of the doubt as he handled a worse situation better than Rian did. The odds were more unfavorable to George as Hollywood had a bone to pick with him for being an outsider that made it big. He faced sharper criticism than Rian did as it came from both the media and fandom. George would also respond to criticism by making adjustments (less Jar Jar and more realistic combat for RoTS). Compare that to Rian Johnson, who was shielded by the media from criticism. He would refuse to take any and all forms of criticism due to ego and responded only with vitriol. What is Rian's excuse?

author: @freakazoid1980
text:
When i was 12yrs old i started watching the original trilogy. Every morning for a year before i went to school, i woke up and watched 10-15mins of empire. 
Later on when the prequels got released, i didn't feel the same excitement and captivation that i felt with the originals. But over the years it grew on me, it became a part of "my" star wars.
When the sequels hit the theaters, i watched the very first movie two times. First time at the premiere night. I left the theater and it felt to me like it was a remastered episode 4. Later i gave it another shot, when i bought it on prime and watched it at home. But i still felt the same way.
When the second movie came out, i went to the premiere and left infuriated, angry how a company with that much money and imo "a general understanding of sw" could release such a thing. To me it almost felt like a spoof movie of star wars. I watched it once and never again.
And ofc i went to the premiere of the 3rd movie. When i left, i wasn't even angry about the movie or disney, i was angry at myself for giving this franchise money that i worked for. And again, i never watched it again.
In conclusion, since i never "consumed" the sequels like i did with the originals, these movies, this canon doesn't "exist" for me. So whenever i see a video like this, with scenes of the sequels, i catch myself thinking "when did they do th...ohhh yeah it actually WAS in the movies". #notmystarwars

replies.text:
@PandaPanda-ud4ne I often doubt the merit of provocation in selling art, because art should be able to sell itself on its own merits without needing to drum up controversy. It seems to me that the only good place for it is in advocacy for some kind of social change, like how _Uncle Tom’s Cabin_ was used to illustrate to the willfully ignorant that even at its “gentlest”, sl-very is cruel and evil. In that case, it wasn’t controversy or provocation about the subject that was used to sell the book, but the book was used to illustrate the controversial and provocative subject. That being said, I can’t think of a reason to use provocation art to make dumb, senseless murder mystery movies, let alone Star Wars of all things. What a dunce

And yes, hard agree that yoko ono sucks

text:
I'm not being hyperbolic here when I say this, but I used to love Star Wars, I wasn't a hardcore fan, but I loved the world and the fantasy and the stories in the universe. Deep down I enjoyed the journey of Luke and yearned to see him rise up one more time as a hero of the galaxy. Then along came TLJ...it literally killed that spark of Star Wars in me, any remnant of my childhood left for it was removed completely from The Last Jedi. 
It was a turning point not only for me in Star Wars, but in Hollywood in general because since then they have just churned out slop even in the MCU. 

The true murder mystery is how did they all manage to murder franchises that were printing money in less time than it took to build the empires?

author: @ericgwalsh
text:
The great thing about the Star Wars trilogy of trilogies is that the marked decline is so evident.

The genius of the 1st trilogy is that it was powerful enough to carry the very good but strained 2nd trilogy and by comparison to the 3rd trilogy, revealed what an utter dogs breakfast the 3rd was.

Reminds me of the drawing of the horse, the head and shoulder is a clear work of art, the middle of the horse is a sketch but still very good, but the hindlegs are a 5 year old scribbling.

They can never escape their abject failure so clearly illustrated for posterity. 

Emblematic of the times.

replies.author: @pohjanakka4992
replies.text:
@ForceMaximus84 I didn't like TFA, it was boring as a story and already started the character assassinations with Han Solo and Chewbacca. But yes, it still might have worked if the second movie had had some real but likable surprises. Like that maybe Han and Leia hadn't actually separated but Han had been a on secret mission of some kind, but got diverted when he found that he would be able to get to their son. That maybe there were more than just that one next generation Skywalker around, the other, or others, having been hidden (as I really hated them killing everyone single one in the Skywalker lineage - to me SW is and was and always will be a family saga, and with none of that family left...). That maybe Luke wasn't "hiding away" but also on some important mission of his own. And so on. 

Maybe give us a flashback scene of the three of them together, figuring what was going wrong with the New Republic and what exactly that new threat Luke had maybe seen in visions was, an planning how to avoid the destruction Luke had also seen, so that they would not end where they do in the end of the actually made movie, with a small, very small, resistance group running away from the New Order and the New Republic destroyed. 

Maybe even use that idea that Rey is, yes, a Palpatine and so on in the story. Make the heroes run through a very tight and twisty situation as they try to execute their plan, and leave the success or failure of their plan hanging on a thread in the end of that movie, maybe hanging on how Rey can deal with the revelation of her true parentage, and whether Ben Solo can be brought back from the dark side, in order to give it a similar structure to the first trilogy (yes yes, repeat of the old, but that they probably would have done anyway). Make Rey a lot more unpredictable and volatile and less reliable, somebody who truly was hanging by a thread to the light side while being way too powerful for her own good with the Force. 

I don't think it would have been possible to make an actually good trilogy starting from TFA, but it certainly would have been possible to make something a lot better than what they actually made.

replies.author: @willdabeastIam
replies.text:
I WISH I'd walked out, but I saw it with my then girlfriend and another couple she was friends with. The MOMENT the film opened, I knew I was going to fucking hate it. Why the fuck is this bombing run, IN SPACE, straight outta fucking Memphis Belle. It was just completely non-sensical, insulting to anyone with a modicum of intelligence, and clearly whoever signed off on this knew absolutely nothing about star wars. It...did not get better from there. 

It was excrutiating, and every single goddamn element of it made me livid. Like, I saw kingdom of the crystal skull in theatres. I was like 11 and I knew it was bad. It was disappointing, but it was just a mildly bad film. It didn't take a universe and characters I loved, rape and murder them, commit necrotic acts with their bodies, then flay them and turn them into a series of grotesque lampshades. The last jedi felt like it actively hated me, not just because I was a white male, but simply for loving the series. To date, I caution everyone who hasn't seen it not to, my blood pressure goes up every time I see the poster or hear it mentioned, and I fly into a nearly untameable inner rage when I meet someone who claims to like it. I've gotten in genuinely heated arguments with random strangers at parties over it.
I saw force awakens in theatres on opening night. It wasn't great, but I was so happy to see a new star wars film in theatres (never thought it would happen, wish it hadn't in hindsight) that I didn't care. Atmosphere was electric, whole theatre clapped when the movie was over despite some admissions in the lobby that it wasn't great. Leaving TLJ was...silence. The disappointment was palpable.
I was told the aforementioned boyfriend of ex-girlfriend's friend was a big star wars fan, and we'd get along great. We...did not. I loved that series since I was 6, whereas he was more of a...funko pops enjoyer. Dude had definitely sampled soylent. Anyways, we leave the theatre, I'm standing there gutted, and he starts going on about how brilliant it was. "..totally subverts audience expectations, strong feminist principles..." I just told him that neither of those things had any bearing on whether or not the film was good, and it was in fact one of the worst pieces of dogshit I'd ever seen, and I felt like my childhood just got raped. Lengthy argument ensued, I left.

Friends tried to get me to to with them the next night. I refused, tried to talk them out of it, met up with them after, and we consumed a 40oz of whiskey between the 3 of us and shit on the movie till the sun came up. 

Fuuuuuuck, this is like a goddamn nam flashback, I hadn't thought about this in depth for years. I don't think I've ever hated a piece of media as much as this film, and I hope our boy Rian one day is beaten to death by the prosthetic cock of a man in a rubber jar jar binks costume. And I hope Kathleen Kennedy stumbles on it, slips on the blood, and has a Life Alert moment. And that whoever is on the other end of the line has  had the misfortune of seeing this fucking hateful film, and does what is necessary.

replies.author: @moutachiang7669
replies.text:
That's just the thing.  Luke saw Kylo's heart and saw a good chance of him becoming baby hitler, so he had a moral dilemma on his hands.  He had the intention of killing baby hitler but he waffles just long enough for Kylo to wake up and be traumatized into the dark side.  After this major eff-up he re-evaluate and decided that since the jedi are not infallible, the world may be better off without them.  I don't think this is inconsistent with his character at all, you can see the good in a supposedly evil person, and evil in a good person.  A lot can happen in a few decades, and while I think more of this character work should have been done I don't think people should cling to the old luke.  Even then, I thought his interactions with Rey when they're training was great.  The scene where he came to face off against Kylo one last time was epic. He showed Kylo who the master was without even touching him.

There was a strong theme in this movie about not clinging to the old ways.  Kylo said it explicitly.  The choice to have Rey's parents to be nobodies was also a very good one, which echoes the last scene where the cleaning kid could just use the force, because anybody could be force sensitive.  If we go back to the beginning, Anakin was a nobody too and he was able to become powerful and complete the circle.  They were going for a break from nostalgia. 

Besides the casino arc nonsense, I really liked the last jedi.  I am probably a minority here, but I think they should have gone balls to the walls and not have backpedaled in the last skywalker.  That's their real sin.

I disagree with Drinker on this one, which is a rare occurrence, but I don't think Rian Johnson fares particularly poorly amongst the Star Wars directors.  I would put his movie above average, actually, around return of the jedi and revenge of the sith.

replies.author: @TheChristianPsychopath
replies.text:
​@Zoltan1251I am a writer.  I study the craft intently.  There are films and shows I dislike but recognize they are well written.  The Last Jedi is not well written.

Rey asks Kylo "Why do you hate your father?"  Kylo responds with "I don't hate my father." ... and the matter is dropped without further discussion.  Kylo had killed his father in cold blood in the last film, so... Where was the explanation for why?  This was a chance to explain or justify himself.

There was an explanation given in Legacy of the Force where Lumiya tricks Jacen Solo with a false prophecy about needing to kill a loved one as some kind of Sith ritual, but that was decanonized.  It wasn't good writing either, but at least it was clear how Jacen was justifying and wrestling with it.  The motivation was dumb but clear.  And even then, he waits until it can be justified as self defense.  ( He kills someone else and that was out of character to make the plot happen, but it at least gives him clear motivation.)

Kylo's motivation to kill Han is never explained.  The audience is left to guess.

Likewise the story about Luke standing over him with a lightsaber does not explain his fall to the darkside, because Luke is reacting to how far Kylo has fallen to the darkside.

It explains why he would get away from Luke.  It doesn't explain why he became a neo nazi obsessed with his grandfather who he had never met.

Other than wanting to be just like Luke Skywalker, redeeming Kylo like Luke redeemed Vader, Rey isn't given much motivation to completely abandon her friends to go try to save the man who tortured her and committed two genocides.  Kylo makes no hint of wanting redemption, and he dodges most of her questions.

It's dramatic, but that's it.  Johnson confuses subverting expectations with good writing, but that's not good writing.  Twists should still make sense within the context of the story.

Same with Abrams and mystery boxes... Yes, it's good to give the reader/viewer questions.  But you need to have answers to the questions that make sense in context.

The unanswered questions and twists will only surprise the audience once, but fail to be satisfying on a rewatch or reflection unless the story itself has a logical flow.

replies.text: I had a really good friend who was massive star wars fan. He was obessed with it.  He died from drinking himself to death, after the pandemic left him alone in his house, and he went crazy as his wife just abandoned him and took their dog with her. I can't help but think may be if Star Wars was actually good, may be that small thing to look forward to might given him something else to live for. It sucking definitely didn't help him.

replies.author: @TheChristianPsychopath
replies.text:
Yeah, I had already kind of broken up with Star Wars after Star by Star drained all the fun out of it for me.  Had tried to return until Legacy of the Force stamped out any remaining hope.  So I gave the reboot a chance... had a tiny moment of hope when they said they were doing something "for the fans" we might get to see Jaina, Jacen and Anakin Solo on screen.... the bleakness of the Del Rey years wiped away.  But having their existence denied and all the good that was in the EU thrown in the garbage was much worse.

Legacy of the Force and Dark Empire were reasons to reboot, not the material a reboot should have been made from.  Now, Legacy of the Force reads like Tolkien in comparison and Dark Empire is relatively Shakespeare... so I have gone back and I'm exploring all the EU material from the early 2000s that I hadn't gotten to.

And no mistake, Legacy of the Force is a dumpster fire of out of character decisions to make the plot happen.  Still infinitely superior to the Disney Sequels.

TFA was not good but salvageable.  TLJ was the first time I felt like a film actively hated me.

The attitude more than anything.  Lucasfilm had been so good to the fans before the Disney sale.  Gold tier fan interactions.  George had gone from hating fan films to see the passion and actually hosting fan film contests.  For a while they even hosted fan websites.  The middle finger to the fandom that was The Last Jedi was just baffling.

author: @noelburland7169
text:
As much as Rian Johnson stuffed up the triology with TLJ, it has to be remembered that Kathleen Kennedy had the creative control over the direction they were going, so she was equally at fault for the disaster that was not just TLJ but also the entire trilogy. That trilogy was made not to continue George Lucas' story but rather to shit all over the legacy of the Original Trilogy, because she wanted her flawless Girl Boss to become the true saviour of the galaxy.

JJ Abrams played it safe with TFA, basically making it into A New Hope but in a flashy new outfit, then Rian Johnson attempted to deconstruct the mythology and subvert expectations with TLJ. Realizing the backlash to TLJ, it seems behind the scenes they were thrust into panic mode and cobbled together a giant mess of a film in TROS, in a failed attempt to salvage the triolgy from the train wreck it became. This is why they resurrected Sheev Palpatine after Johnson casually killed off Supreme Leader Snoke in TLJ, complete with that comical line: "Somehow Palpatine returned". In doing that they undermined the legacies of Anakin and Luke Skywalker in a giant slap to their faces, with the flawless Girl Boss even stealing the Skywalker name at the end of the film in a final insult.

replies.author: @JacobGforce
replies.text: Yeah, that fact alone (stealing Luke’s and Anakin’s victory away and without even hinting at how Palpatine survived until now) made the sequels utterly dead to me. It was all a bad dream and you can’t convince me otherwise.

replies.author: @DW-zg2oy
replies.text: @JacobGforce If I ever was forced to acknowledge that trilogy, I'd refer to it as the "Gas Leak Trilogy". (Community reference)

replies.author: @noelburland7169
replies.text:
​​@JacobGforcethere are stories in the EU where Palpatine's spirit lingered on long after his physical body was destroyed. There are also probably stories about him attempting to clone his body too. I guess the writers lazily cherry picked from these stories to say he was pulling the strings all along and Snoke was nothing more than a clone and a puppet for the First Order.

They ended up doing this simply because there was no cohesive plan set up from the get-go to map out the trilogy. When Rian Johnson tossed Snoke aside like he was meaningless the writers had to scramble to have a big baddie for Rey to take down and the only logical thing they could do was bring Palpatine back and hope that people wouldn't question their logic lol.

Imagine you've had a mom that would make delicious chocolate cakes in your childhood with an unique recipe that you are nostalgic of, and which use to be a delecacy for all your family

Episode 7 was like serving an industrial pre-cooked chocolate cake, trying to find the same taste in it, it's "the same thing", but it's lazy, unoriginal, not nearly as good, and frankly not very honest. 

Ryan johnson is the guy that would see this industrial cake, see it for the fraud it was and be like "fuck this", take a baseball bat and smash it flat, put some candies and weird stuff on it and gloat in his genius for saving gastronomy. 

And episode 9 would be you, trying to fix the now near destroyed cake somehow.

author: @msullivan85
text: It was such a huge part of my childhood.  I used to buy the toys, the merchandise.  I'd read about the EU.  I played the hell out of KOTOR 1 & 2.  It's all gone.  I cringe anytime Star Wars is mentioned.  These people took rabid built in fanbase, a framework that had already been established, a legacy that had integrated itself into the culture spanning literal decades.........and threw it all in the trash.  It's almost inconceivable.  You would have to be trying to screw up, not to be able to capitalize on that.  They didn't even bother laying out a plot for a trilogy in the writer's room.  That's the craziest thing I ever heard about the production.  They didn't even have a fleshed-out idea for 3 movies.  HTF can that happen!?  Never mind some of them had never even seen the originals!  I've never watched the Twilight series of movies.  It's not my thing but you better believe I'd watch every single one if somebody was paying me to write a f***ing sequel for them.

text: On a serious note, I think the Last Jedi was the best 'made' of the sequels. I still love the throne room sequence. But I don't know that Kennedy or Jonhson really get why this movie in particular, but really the whole trilogy 'failed.' (Obviously not financially). The trilogy went off the rails when Starkiller base fired its nonsensical laser and destroyed the New Republic before we got to know squat about it. Up until then, the Force Awakens was nailing every single note. I liked the new characters. They were interesting. But the problem is they turned our OG heroes into losers. With the Last Jedi taking a big fat dump on the character of Luke Skywalker. Per the canon of the sequels, here is the legacy of our heroes: Leia was terrible at governance and not a particularly good military leader. Han was a deadbeat Dad. And Luke was an all time terrible Jedi Master, never mind uncle. F*** them for doing that to those three characters. Subversive my behind.
